turns.content: I've been back in the game for five years now and I keep hitting a wall with proptest. I read about implementing Arbitrary but it feels like magic to me, specifically when dealing with custom domain types.

It's tricky because you have to implement the Arbitrary trait for your struct manually instead of relying on a macro to generate everything. Think of it like building a specific key for a lock; you can't just use a generic one, you must craft the sequence that matches exactly how that type gets constructed.
So if I have a User struct with fields like id and email, do I just write Arbitrary for each field and then combine them?

Exactly. You implement the trait on your User struct by calling into the strategies of its inner types using the .just() method. For primitive types like u32 or String, proptest provides standard implementations you can simply call.
What about when I have a complex nested structure? Like an Option inside a Vec?

You need to define custom strategies for those specific types. For the Option, you can use proptest::arbitrary::any::<Option<T>>() with a predicate that ensures valid data, or just call .just() on your inner type if it's already implemented.
Wait, I thought predicates were only for filtering test cases? How do they fit into Arbitrary?

In this context, a predicate acts as a guard to ensure the data generated by the strategy meets your business logic requirements. You pass it into .just() so that only valid instances of your type get tested.
Okay so I can write my own Arbitrary impl for a custom enum? Like Status which has Pending and Completed states?
Yes, absolutely. You define the impl block with the trait bound on your specific type and then use .just() to generate instances of each variant.
Let me try to write this out in my IDE now...
Make sure you import the trait and any helper macros from proptest. You might also want to check if your custom types implement traits like Debug or Clone so they can be used effectively in tests.

I just realized I was trying to use a macro for the whole struct, but it failed because my enum variants had different field requirements. Now that I know I need manual impls with custom strategies, it makes perfect sense.
That's the key insight. Manual implementation gives you full control over how each variant is generated, which is why arbitrary testing shines for complex domain models.
